skill.name: protocolsio-integration
skill.description: Use protocols.io API guidance to search, create, update, publish, discuss, organize, upload, download, and export scientific protocol content.
skill.summary: Manage protocols.io protocols, workspaces, discussions, files, experiments, and exports through API workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution and blocker patterns, but review found these are markdown code fences, curl examples, placeholder API tokens, and keyword collisions such as description, desired, and includes. The skill is not malicious, but it intentionally guides authenticated protocols.io API calls that can upload, download, publish, delete, and export research content, so users need a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated API Operations Can Modify Research Content
medium_findings.description: The skill documents authenticated protocols.io operations for creating protocols, adding steps, publishing protocols, and changing user-owned content. This is intended functionality, but misuse can publish or alter scientific records and should require user confirmation before write actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced sections explicitly show authenticated create and publish workflows. This is legitimate API guidance, but the operations have durable user-visible effects.

medium_findings.title: File Transfer and Organization Workflows Handle User Data
medium_findings.description: The skill includes workflows for uploading local files, downloading workspace files, deleting files, and exporting organization data. These operations can expose private research files if credentials, workspace IDs, or file selections are wrong.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file upload, download, deletion, and organization export examples are concrete and authenticated. They are expected for the skill, but they carry data handling risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Runtime Backticks
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts are false positives caused by markdown inline code and fenced examples. The skill files are documentation and do not contain executable Ruby backtick calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax is inside markdown documentation and shell examples, not application code. No Ruby source file or runtime command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword Collisions
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography alerts are false positives from words such as description, desired, and includes. No MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic implementation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found ordinary API parameter names and prose at representative flagged lines. There is no evidence of weak cryptographic code or advice.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Token Examples Require Secret Hygiene
low_findings.description: Authentication examples use placeholder bearer tokens and advise secure storage. The examples are safe as written, but users should avoid pasting real tokens into prompts, logs, or shared files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows placeholder credentials and explicit best practices. The risk depends on user handling of real tokens rather than malicious skill behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Network Request Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides curl and Python requests to protocols.io with bearer-token authentication. These requests are expected for the integration but should be run only after confirming the target workspace, protocol, and action.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Network calls are explicitly documented and use the official protocols.io API base URL. They are not covert, but authenticated calls can affect user data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive API Operations Documented
dangerous_patterns.description: The references include delete operations for protocol steps, comments, experiment records, and files. These are valid API functions, but agents should ask before executing destructive requests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The files document destructive endpoints, but no automatic execution is present. Risk is controlled by user intent and confirmation.

content.value_statement: Scientific teams need reliable ways to find, update, publish, and archive protocols without losing context. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured protocols.io API guidance for lab workflow automation.
actual_capabilities: Explains protocols.io API authentication with client tokens, OAuth tokens, refresh tokens, and rate limits.
Guides protocol search, retrieval, creation, update, step management, publishing, bookmarking, and PDF export.
Covers discussion workflows for protocol comments, threaded replies, step comments, edits, and deletions.
Describes workspace access, member roles, workspace protocol lists, and team collaboration workflows.
Provides file manager workflows for uploads, downloads, metadata updates, folders, deletion, restoration, and backups.
Documents profile, publication discovery, experiment run, notification, and organization export endpoints.
limitations: Does not provide a packaged API client, executable script, or installed command-line tool.
Requires valid protocols.io API credentials and permissions for private or write operations.
API behavior, endpoint availability, and rate limits may change outside the skill content.
Does not validate scientific correctness, safety, or regulatory suitability of protocol content.

use_cases.title: Find and Cite Public Protocols
use_cases.description: Search public protocols by topic, review metadata and comments, bookmark useful results, and prepare DOI-based citations.

use_cases.title: Maintain Lab Protocol Libraries
use_cases.description: Create workspace protocols, organize files, manage comments, and document updates across shared team repositories.

use_cases.title: Build Internal Research Integrations
use_cases.description: Map protocols.io endpoints into internal tools for protocol import, experiment tracking, notifications, and data export.

prompt_templates.title: Search for Protocols
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me search protocols.io for public CRISPR protocols. Show the endpoint, required parameters, and a safe review checklist before I save any results.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Draft Protocol
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a protocols.io API workflow to create a draft protocol with title, description, tags, and ordered steps. Do not publish it yet.

prompt_templates.title: Organize Workspace Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a workspace file organization workflow for uploading experiment data, adding metadata, verifying upload status, and linking files to protocols.

prompt_templates.title: Automate Lab Reporting
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an end-to-end integration that imports protocol details, tracks experiment runs, checks comments, exports organization data, and handles rate limits safely.

output_examples.input: Find protocols for a CRISPR literature review.
output_examples.output: A search plan with public publication filters, review steps for protocol details and comments, and citation notes for DOI-based references.
A backup checklist covering workspace listing, folder traversal, file download, metadata preservation, access review, and secure storage.
A structured workflow for documenting runs, linking result files, recording modifications, and comparing success rates across repeated experiments.

best_practices: Confirm workspace, protocol, and file identifiers before any write, publish, delete, or export action.
Store access tokens outside prompts, source control, shared notebooks, and visible command history.
Respect protocols.io rate limits and use retry logic with exponential backoff for transient failures.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real bearer tokens into chat prompts, examples, logs, or public repositories.
Do not publish protocols or issue DOI updates until scientific review and ownership checks are complete.
Do not upload sensitive research data unless workspace visibility and consent requirements are verified.

faq.question: Does this skill include a working protocols.io API client?
faq.answer: No. It provides endpoint guidance, workflows, examples, and implementation patterns for building or using API requests.

faq.question: Which credentials are required?
faq.answer: You need a protocols.io client access token or OAuth access token with permissions for the target content.

faq.question: Can it publish protocols and create DOIs?
faq.answer: It documents the publish workflow. Publishing should require explicit user confirmation because the result can be public and durable.

faq.question: Can it manage private workspace files?
faq.answer: Yes, if the authenticated user has workspace permissions. Treat file uploads, downloads, exports, and deletions as sensitive operations.

faq.question: Does it check scientific safety?
faq.answer: No. Users must review protocol content for scientific accuracy, laboratory safety, ethics, and compliance requirements.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate documentation, but it guides authenticated network and file operations that can modify or expose research data.
